vitals.location: Pune to Bogmallo Beach, Goa, India
vitals.field_size: ~65 solo riders + 8 relay teams (2025 roster, ~89 listed cyclists)
vitals.start_format: Nonstop point-to-point ultra; solo self-supported, solo crew-supported, and 2-/3-/4-person relay categories
vitals.feed_zones: Organizer-arranged midpoint drop-bag support (self-supported categories); full crew support (supported categories)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 66. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 63. FLAG (2026-07-24 editorial wave 7): major vitals correction — this is a 643km/5,907m ultra-distance race (organizer-confirmed), not the 120.7km/null-elevation entry previously on file, which described only a fraction of the actual event. distance=2, climbing=4, and every other dimension were scored against wildly wrong course facts; recommend a full human re-score against the actual 643km ultra format (see catalog_flags taxonomy note). Numbers left untouched per no-silent-reshuffle rule.

course_description.character: A nonstop, point-to-point ultra-distance race across the Deccan Plateau and the Sahyadris (Western Ghats), starting at Keshavbaug, DP Road, Karve Nagar, Pune, and finishing 643 km later at Bogmallo Beach, Goa. Mahabaleshwar/Panchgani is only an early waypoint on the route (via the Pasarni Ghat), not the finish — this database previously described the race as ending there. The route also passes Satara, Karad, Kolhapur and Belgaum before a forested descent into Goa, entirely on paved public roads and highways.
course_description.signature_challenge: The full 643 km distance itself — a nonstop RAAM-qualifier ultra with no shorter competitive category. All formats (solo self-supported, solo crew-supported, and relay teams of 2-4) ride the same course.
suffering_zones: Katraj/Khambatki — early Ghat climbing out of Pune
Panchgani/Pasarni Ghat — the Mahabaleshwar-area climbing this profile previously (and wrongly) treated as the finish
The Satara-Karad-Kolhapur-Belgaum corridor — long sustained distance on the Deccan Plateau
The forested descent into Goa — technical descending after 600+ km of accumulated fatigue

logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels and guesthouses abundant in Pune and Goa; solo self-supported riders rely on organizer-arranged midpoint drop-bag support rather than a personal follow vehicle.
logistics.transport: Start: Keshavbaug, DP Road, Karve Nagar, Pune. Finish: Bogmallo Beach, Goa.

history.origin_story: Founded in 2014 (initially held in February) by organizer Inspire India. The solo categories operate as Race Across America (RAAM) qualifiers.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Serious Indian Ultra, Not a Sportive
biased_opinion.summary: Deccan Cliffhanger is a genuine, established Indian ultracycling race — a nonstop 643 km/5,907m point-to-point from Pune to Goa across the Deccan Plateau and Western Ghats, running since 2014 with real RAAM-qualifier stakes for its solo categories. That's a fundamentally different and far more demanding event than the 120.7 km sportive-style entry this database previously carried, which appears to have captured only an early fragment of the route. Every rider covers the same full 643 km, whether solo self-supported, solo crew-supported, or as part of a relay team — there is no shorter competitive category to ease into.
strengths: Established, continuously run Indian ultra since 2014 with genuine RAAM-qualifier stakes
Consequential, varied terrain across the Western Ghats and Deccan Plateau, finishing at the Goa coast
Both self-supported and crew-supported solo pathways, plus 2-/3-/4-person relay options that broaden accessibility below the full solo distance
A real, credible organizer (Inspire India) with over a decade of continuity
weaknesses: Exceptionally demanding — 643 km nonstop with no shorter competitive category, unlike a conventional gran fondo's multi-distance options
Traffic, night riding, and variable road conditions on Indian highways are a real safety factor over such distance
Small field (roughly 65 solo riders plus 8 relay teams in 2025) and no current entry-fee transparency
Crew-supported entries require substantial logistics beyond what most riders bring to a sportive-style event
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A legitimate, serious ultracycling institution that has nothing in common with the sportive-style entry this profile used to describe — score, market, and prepare for it as a 643 km ultra, not a 120 km fondo.

final_verdict.one_liner: A 643km/5,907m nonstop Pune-to-Goa RAAM-qualifier ultra — not the 120km sportive this profile previously described.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you're specifically seeking a serious ultracycling RAAM-qualifier challenge across the Western Ghats. Not a fit if you're looking for a conventional single-day gran fondo — there's no shorter competitive category here.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a comparable Indian road ultra, look at other RAAM-qualifier events on the subcontinent; for a conventional single-day Indian road sportive, look elsewhere in the catalog.

catalog_flags.status_note: Taxonomy note: this is a nonstop, self-supported/crew-supported ultracycling RAAM qualifier (643km/5,907m), not a conventional gran fondo. Not changing the discipline enum per editorial-wave scope — flagging for a human taxonomy decision.
